picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO just delivered its sixth consecutive double beat: Q2 FY2026 revenue of $22.2B (+48% YoY), AI semiconductor revenue of $10.8B (+143% YoY), and Q3 guidance of $29.4B with >200% AI segment growth — all above consensus. The post-earnings dip driven by a minor software segment miss is a thesis-confirming buy-the-dip opportunity, not a structural break. Custom XPU model creates sticky multi-year hyperscaler relationships with an AI backlog above $70B and multi-year deals extending to 2031, precisely the durable revenue visibility this regime demands.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (Sept 2026) validating >200% AI semiconductor revenue guide of $16B; custom AI chip revenue trajectory toward $100B annually by end of 2027
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3 months (Q3 results) and 12–18 months (custom silicon ramp)
picks.entryNote: Post-earnings dip to high-30s forward P/E after a software segment miss — well below the pre-earnings run. Trading below recent highs on elevated volume suggesting institutional accumulation; AI backlog >$70B provides multi-quarter revenue floor.
picks.exitTrigger: Any two hyperscaler customers announcing a meaningful reduction in custom XPU design win commitments, or AI semiconductor segment growth decelerating below 80% YoY for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex cycle deceleration — if Microsoft or Google trims AI infrastructure spend, AVGO custom silicon orders are the first to see cancellations

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Communication Services saw Q1 2026 earnings growth revised from -3.7% to +48.9% — the largest upward earnings revision of any sector — driven primarily by Alphabet monetizing AI at scale across Search, Cloud, and YouTube. Berkshire/Abel tripled its GOOGL stake to ~57.8M shares (~$16.6B), making it the 5th-largest Berkshire holding — the strongest smart money endorsement in the Q1 13F cycle. Google's $190B AI buildout for 2026 is a direct tailwind for its own cloud infrastructure and a Marvell custom silicon order pipeline.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — consensus expects continued AI-driven Search revenue acceleration and Google Cloud margin expansion above 17%
picks.entryNote: Carried from prior days at high conviction; Communication Services XLC +15.1% YoY revenue growth with Interactive Media & Services (GOOGL-dominant sub-sector) at +24% YoY. Trades at a meaningful discount to MSFT on forward P/E despite comparable AI monetization trajectory.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud market share loss to Azure/AWS for two consecutive quarters, or Department of Justice search remedies imposing structural separation of Search distribution
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedy (Chrome/Android separation or default search contract prohibition) that structurally impairs Search economics — the single largest binary legal overhang in the portfolio

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA reported Q1 FY2027 EPS of $1.87 (+95% YoY), beating estimates by 6.25%, with Blackwell architecture ramping across hyperscalers, enterprises, and sovereign AI customers — extending the demand cycle well beyond the original hyperscaler-only thesis. Combined hyperscaler capex of ~$800B in 2026 rising toward $1.16T in 2027 means the addressable market is still expanding. Jensen Huang's endorsement of MRVL as the 'next trillion-dollar company' at Computex reflects NVDA's ecosystem orchestration power, not competitive pressure.
picks.catalyst: Blackwell Ultra (B300) production ramp confirmation at GTC or partner events; any NVDA-China export license relaxation from US-China AI summit
picks.entryNote: Reduced to #3 (from #2 on May 28) given post-Computex valuation extension; at ~30x forward earnings on elevated consensus, the risk/reward is less asymmetric than AVGO at current levels. Hold conviction sizing but below AVGO weight. Trading near recent highs with extension flag acknowledged.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler capex cuts of >15% across two or more of Microsoft/Google/Amazon, or US export controls effectively eliminating China data center revenue (>$15B annualized)
picks.keyRisk: AI capex cycle deceleration or open-source model efficiency gains that reduce the inference compute intensity per dollar of revenue generated by hyperscalers

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the convergence of the two strongest forces in the portfolio: AI infrastructure (AWS, tracking toward ~$190B capex in 2026 alongside Microsoft) and consumer resilience in a value-conscious macro environment. David Tepper nearly doubled his AMZN stake to 4.3M shares (~$900M, 15.2% of Appaloosa's portfolio), and Bill Ackman added ~1.84M shares making it his #2 position. Tepper's thesis — that Amazon's elevated AI capex is a long-term strategic investment supporting AWS expansion — is the right read for a 1-3 year horizon.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — AWS margin trajectory above 38% operating margin is the single most watched datapoint; advertising revenue re-acceleration is the second
picks.entryNote: Held continuously at high conviction for 7+ trading days. AMZN is the portfolio's most diversified growth name: e-commerce, AWS, advertising, and logistics each provide independent earnings streams, reducing single-theme concentration risk vs. pure-play AI names.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS operating margin declining below 34% for two consecutive quarters, or meaningful market share loss in cloud to Azure driven by Microsoft's Copilot bundling advantage
picks.keyRisk: AWS capex cycle producing margin compression faster than revenue acceleration — if $100B+ annual infrastructure spend doesn't convert to proportional revenue within 3 years, the multiple corrects sharply

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle is the highest-conviction AI infrastructure name with the cleanest near-term catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings due in approximately 2 weeks, with Scotiabank raising its price target to $290 and UBS to $285 in the past 48 hours. OCI (Oracle Cloud Infrastructure) growth >50% YoY driven by OpenAI and Anthropic as new anchor tenants sets a durable multi-year demand floor. The momentum screen rates ORCL 20/25 with 4/4 EPS beat streak, and it was added to the growth book on June 3 replacing VST as #9.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (mid-June 2026) — consensus watching for OCI revenue run rate, RPO acceleration above $100B, and any new hyperscaler co-location contract announcements
picks.entryNote: Scotiabank PT raised to $290 from $215 and UBS to $285 from $250 this week — analysts front-running an earnings beat. Trades at ~32x 2026 EPS estimates with 5-year CAGR modeled above 37% on asset-light cloud model. Stock slid modestly into earnings, offering a favorable setup vs. elevated expectations.
picks.exitTrigger: OCI revenue growth decelerating below 35% YoY or RPO growth reversal, signaling hyperscaler customers are consolidating on AWS/Azure rather than Oracle's sovereign cloud
picks.keyRisk: OpenAI and Anthropic reducing Oracle data center commitments in favor of hyperscaler-owned infrastructure as Microsoft and Google scale their own AI compute capacity

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the cleanest expression of capital markets re-opening in a late-cycle regime: 3/4 EPS beat rate with FY26 estimates revised up ~8% over 13 weeks on M&A advisory pipeline recovery, IPO calendar reopening, and strong trading revenue from Iran-conflict-driven energy volatility. Financials sector Q1 2026 revenue grew +10.9% YoY and the sector trades at a discount to the S&P 500 on forward P/E. The steepening yield curve (+47bps 2s10s) provides a structural NIM tailwind into 2H 2026.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — investment banking revenue line is the key watch; any sequential acceleration in M&A advisory fees signals the deal cycle is fully reopened
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. Carries among the best ROIC trajectory and P/B vs. ROTCE within mega-cap financials. Rate environment (3.50-3.75% funds rate, 4.45% 10-year) is constructive for Goldman's fixed income trading franchise without crushing its deal advisory business.
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenue declining more than 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or a credit market dislocation (HY spreads >500bps) that freezes the capital markets pipeline entirely
picks.keyRisk: A hawkish Fed surprise at the June 16-17 FOMC (rate hike signal) that freezes the M&A/IPO pipeline and pressures Goldman's equity underwriting backlog

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX sits at the intersection of two of the most durable spending mega-trends in the portfolio: NATO/global defense budget expansion (driven by geopolitical rearmament) and commercial aerospace recovery (Pratt & Whitney GTF engine aftermarket). Industrial stocks have gained more than 16% YTD in 2026, and defense/aerospace names are explicitly rated as top Tier 1 contributors in the sector rotation scorecard. RTX's ROIC trajectory is improving as GTF engine remediation costs roll off through 2026, unlocking meaningful FCF re-acceleration.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — GTF engine remediation cost run-rate update and Raytheon defense bookings backlog growth are the two key catalysts
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. XLI 1-year total return of +25.66% confirms sector leadership. RTX specifically benefits from Iran-conflict-driven defense spending acceleration and a multi-year commercial aerospace aftermarket tailwind independent of macro.
picks.exitTrigger: GTF engine remediation costs materially exceeding $10B total reserve, or a sustained ceasefire in both Middle East and Ukraine simultaneously reducing the geopolitical defense premium
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine inspection scope expanding beyond current estimates, introducing a new round of negative EPS revisions and cash outflows at Pratt & Whitney that compress the FCF re-acceleration thesis

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Energy is the only S&P 500 sector trading at a discount both to the overall index and to its own 10-year average — roughly 32% cheap on each measure — while energy stocks have surged more than 22% YTD in 2026 on Iran-conflict-driven oil prices. Brent crude remains above $100/barrel and the PCE energy component is driving the 3.5% total PCE reading. XOM is the largest single contributor to energy sector YTD gains alongside Chevron, with a Permian Basin low-cost production base that generates strong FCF at $70+ oil and extraordinary returns at $100+.
picks.catalyst: May CPI (June 11) confirming energy price pass-through; OPEC+ summer production decision; any Iran de-escalation creating a buy-the-dip entry at $70–80 oil where XOM still generates 8%+ FCF yield
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks (CPI + OPEC) and 6–12 months (geopolitical resolution/escalation binary)
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. Morgan Stanley base case assumes crude returns to ~$90/barrel by year-end, still supportive of XOM's FCF. Berkshire trimmed Chevron ~35% in Q1 but XOM's Permian integration and downstream complexity differentiate it from a pure upstream commodity play.
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained oil price decline below $65/barrel on confirmed Hormuz reopening plus global demand destruction, or an IEA report showing peak oil demand pull-forward into 2025
picks.keyRisk: Binary geopolitical resolution — a genuine Iran diplomatic deal could drop oil 10-15% rapidly, compressing near-term Energy sector EPS revisions before volume benefits materialize

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a secular compounder at the intersection of data network effects, credit market activity, and ESG/index expansion — the highest-quality Financials name in the universe. With IG and HY issuance re-accelerating as the yield curve steepens and M&A advisory pipelines reopen, Ratings segment revenue is directly leveraged to deal flow. Market Intelligence (Platts, Commodity Insights) benefits from energy market volatility and infrastructure data demand. SPGI's ~75% gross margin and ~$4B+ annual FCF with ROIC above 30% earn it the quality overlay crown in the Financials sector.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Ratings segment revenue acceleration is the key watch given IG issuance activity; any Market Intelligence segment NRR expansion above 105% signals pricing power
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6–8 weeks (Q2 earnings) and ongoing (credit cycle tailwind)
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. SPGI benefits from a high-carry rate environment (float income on Ratings cash) while being structurally immune to credit cycle direction — it earns fees on both issuance and restructuring. Trades at a modest premium to historical averages but justified by >30% ROIC.
picks.exitTrigger: Credit market seizure (HY spreads >600bps) freezing new issuance for more than two quarters, or DOJ/FTC action threatening the Ratings oligopoly structure
picks.keyRisk: A hard landing scenario freezing debt capital markets — if issuance volumes collapse 30%+, Ratings segment revenue faces a cliff that its subscription businesses cannot fully offset

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: Healthcare has underperformed the S&P 500 by ~60 percentage points since 2020 and is now coiling at a 15% forward P/E discount to the broader market — the textbook setup for a mean-reversion long in a late-cycle regime where defensive characteristics gain premium. Merck specifically benefits from Keytruda's multi-year oncology exclusivity runway and a pipeline of ADC (antibody-drug conjugate) assets that have not been priced into current multiples. The FDA has proven its support for a strong biopharma industry with accelerated approval programs, directly reducing Merck's regulatory risk timeline.
picks.catalyst: Keytruda subcutaneous formulation FDA approval (expected H2 2026) and multiple Phase 3 readouts in lung/renal oncology; IRA drug pricing negotiation schedule clarity for 2027
picks.entryNote: Held at medium conviction for 7+ trading days. Policy uncertainty dominated healthcare through 2025 producing historically low relative P/E ratios; investors now see a clearer path around pharmaceutical tariffs and have better FDA clarity. MRK trades at ~14x forward earnings vs. the S&P 500 at 20-21x — one of the most compressed healthcare valuations vs. earnings quality.
picks.exitTrigger: Keytruda Phase 3 combination therapy failure in a major indication, or IRA negotiations expanding to include Keytruda in the 2027 price-negotiation list earlier than expected
picks.keyRisk: Keytruda IRA drug price negotiation in 2027–2028 that structurally impairs the $25B+ annual revenue anchor before pipeline replacements are fully approved and commercialized

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the cleanest macro hedge in a stagflationary late-cycle regime: it performs in both upside inflation surprise (Iran-driven energy re-acceleration) and risk-off flight-to-quality (growth scare/recession). Real assets including gold delivered outstanding performances in early 2026, and with the 30-year yield at the 97th percentile on a 5-year lookback offering minimal term-premium cushion, GLD provides explicit portfolio insurance against both tail scenarios without equity-like drawdown risk.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print (June 11) — a hot read (>3.9% YoY) would validate the inflation-hedge narrative and push gold toward new highs; any Iran re-escalation is an immediate catalyst
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. GLD screens as 'overbought on a relative basis' in the risk assessment, warranting a measured position size (7%) rather than overweight. Dollar strength (DXY bid near recent highs) is the primary headwind to monitor.
picks.exitTrigger: Confirmed Iran ceasefire plus Fed rate-cut cycle resumption simultaneously — a combination that reduces both inflation-premium and recession-fear demand for gold while strengthening risk-on conditions
picks.keyRisk: A rapid DXY appreciation driven by Fed hawkishness (Warsh signals rate hike) compresses gold in USD terms even as real rates remain elevated — the classic dollar-gold negative correlation pressure

picks.name: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides quality-factor exposure through dividend-growth stocks (high ROIC, low leverage, consistent FCF) — precisely the factor that is outperforming in the current late-cycle regime. In a world where the S&P 500 earnings yield is negative versus the 10-year Treasury, SCHD's ~3.5% dividend yield plus quality screen offers positive carry with equity-like upside participation and meaningfully lower beta than the cap-weighted index. It serves as the portfolio's income anchor and diversifier away from high-multiple AI names.
picks.catalyst: Any macro deterioration (weak NFP, soft CPI) that rotates capital from high-multiple growth to quality-value — SCHD is the primary beneficiary of that rotation within equities
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; accelerates on any risk-off episode
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. SCHD's equal-weighted quality tilt aligns with the confirmed outperformance of the equal-weighted S&P 500 (RSP) over the cap-weighted index in 2026 — the 'broadening rotation' flagged in sector updates. Negative 1-year net flows (-$1.79B in XLP as a comparable) suggest institutional underweighting, which supports a contrarian conviction.
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained acceleration of the AI-only growth regime that persistently drives RSP underperformance vs. SPY for 6+ consecutive months, signaling quality/dividend factor is structurally disadvantaged
picks.keyRisk: A melt-up scenario where only high-multiple growth names outperform — in which case SCHD participates modestly in gains but significantly underperforms the portfolio's growth sleeve

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire is the portfolio's fortress: ~$397B in cash/T-bills earning ~3.6% risk-free (>$14B/year in interest income alone), a diversified operating business spanning insurance, energy, rails, and retail, and a beta historically near 0.7 vs. the S&P 500. Abel's Q1 13F moves — tripling GOOGL, exiting payment processors, building cash — reflect disciplined capital allocation and strategic clarity. In a regime where HY spreads are at the 14th percentile (dangerously complacent), Berkshire's fortress balance sheet provides optionality to deploy at distressed prices when credit reprices.
picks.catalyst: Any market dislocation (>10% S&P drawdown) that activates the $397B deployment optionality — historically Berkshire's most powerful return driver is crisis-period buying
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; highest conviction in a risk-off scenario
picks.entryNote: Held at high conviction for 7+ trading days. Trades at ~1.5x book value — modestly above Buffett's historical buyback floor of 1.2x, but the cash hoard itself is understated in book value (T-bills at par vs. mark-to-market optionality value). BRK.B's negative equity risk premium vs. bonds is lower than the S&P 500 given insurance float income.
picks.exitTrigger: Abel deploying the $397B cash hoard into a sector or asset class that is clearly overvalued at the time of purchase — a capital allocation error that destroys the optionality value that justifies the premium
picks.keyRisk: Greg Abel's first major capital allocation decision (acquisition or large equity purchase) being poorly received by markets, triggering a de-rating of the Berkshire management premium from Buffett-era levels

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF is the portfolio's explicit hedge against the growth-scare / recession tail — J.P. Morgan assigns ~35% probability to a hard landing scenario. If the May NFP (tomorrow, June 5) disappoints materially or the June 17 FOMC under Warsh signals a rate-cut restart, the 7-10 year Treasury rallies sharply. Duration of ~7.5 years provides meaningful convexity vs. TLT's excessive sensitivity to a sticky-inflation scenario. The 10-year at 4.45% provides attractive nominal yield in a hold-to-maturity context while offering capital appreciation potential if the growth regime deteriorates.
picks.catalyst: May NFP print (June 5) — a significant miss relative to consensus would immediately reprice the front end lower and IEF would rally 1-2% in a single session
picks.entryNote: Held at medium conviction for 7+ trading days. The 2s10s spread at +47bps is the tightest constructive point — not deeply inverted, but not steep enough to suggest monetary easing imminent. IEF at a 4.4% nominal yield offers the highest risk-free carry it has since 2007-2008 while providing portfolio ballast. Hold at 5% as a deliberate portfolio hedge, not an alpha source.
picks.exitTrigger: May CPI (June 11) printing >0.4% MoM core, confirming the re-acceleration scenario — which would push the 10-year through 4.60-4.70% and impair IEF's price materially
picks.keyRisk: Sticky inflation forces the Warsh Fed to signal a rate hike at June 17 FOMC, driving the 10-year above 4.70% and generating a meaningful mark-to-market loss on the IEF position

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's consumer staples anchor: pricing power demonstrated through 4%+ organic revenue growth in a high-inflation environment, ~3% dividend yield with 62+ consecutive years of dividend growth, and global distribution network that functions as a natural currency hedge across Iran-conflict and dollar-volatility scenarios. In a late-cycle regime where consumer discretionary is explicitly underweighted (XLY Tier 4), KO offers the defensive consumer exposure with zero cyclicality and genuine pricing power — precisely the Consumer Staples quality the scorecard endorses for 'portfolio ballast.'
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth rate vs. volume mix (pricing vs. volume) is the key watch; any sustained volume recovery in international markets is upside
picks.entryNote: Held at medium conviction for 7+ trading days. XLP 3-month net flows are -$1.75B — institutional underweighting creates a re-rating catalyst if macro deteriorates. KO trades at ~24x forward earnings, modestly above its 10-year average but justified by dividend growth consistency and global brand moat. Sized at 3% — ballast, not alpha.
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained volume decline of >5% in North America driven by GLP-1 adoption reducing sugary beverage consumption, combined with an inability to offset through price increases
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 (Mounjaro/Ozempic) secular adoption reducing caloric beverage consumption structurally — a slow-burn but real long-term demand headwind that would require a multi-year portfolio repositioning

summary: The portfolio operates in a confirmed late-cycle stagflationary regime: Fed on hold at 3.50-3.75% for five consecutive meetings, CPI at 3.8% in April with PCE at 3.5%, and the June 16-17 FOMC under new Chair Warsh as the dominant near-term binary event. The 10 growth picks express three compounding themes — AI infrastructure supercycle (AVGO, NVDA, ORCL), AI monetization at scale (GOOGL, AMZN), and late-cycle real-economy quality (GS, RTX, XOM, SPGI, MRK) — while the 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) provides explicit insurance against the three tail risks identified: sticky inflation re-acceleration, AI capex deceleration, and geopolitical shock. All seven names from the prior day's book (AVGO, GOOGL, NVDA, AMZN, GS, RTX, XOM, SPGI) are carried forward with ORCL retained from June 3's addition on the strength of imminent Q4 earnings with Scotiabank/UBS price target upgrades; MRK replaces VST in the medium-conviction healthcare coiling position, and the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is unchanged. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AVGO-style 'software segment miss' moment across multiple AI names if hyperscaler capex cycles pause — managed through AVGO's post-earnings re-entry, GOOGL's ad-revenue diversification, and AMZN's AWS/advertising dual-engine structure.
diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA, ORCL — capped at 3), Communication Services (GOOGL), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX), Energy (XOM), and Health Care (MRK); AVGO and NVDA share AI-infrastructure secular exposure but occupy complementary positions in the value chain (custom XPU fabless design vs. merchant GPU platform). Defensive sleeve spans 4 distinct exposures: commodities/gold inflation hedge (GLD), 7-10yr Treasury duration/recession hedge (IEF), broad dividend-quality equity income (SCHD), diversified operating/cash fortress with equity optionality (BRK.B), and consumer staples pricing-power anchor (KO) — no two defensive positions share the same return driver.

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO is the single highest-conviction idea in the book heading into June 3 Q2 earnings, with company guidance calling for $22B revenue (+47% YoY) and $10.7B AI semiconductor revenue (+140% YoY) — both well above the analyst consensus of ~$20.4-20.5B. Broadcom's custom silicon moat (Google TPUs, Meta AI chips, Microsoft processors) creates deep switching costs and ~77% gross margins, while CEO Hock Tan has stated the company has 'line of sight' to $100B+ in AI chip revenue by 2027. Druckenmiller initiated a new position in Q1 2026, and 35 of 42 covering analysts rate it Strong Buy with a ~$470 average price target.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings report on June 3, 2026 — guided for $22B revenue (+47% YoY) and $10.7B AI revenue (+140% YoY), with Oppenheimer predicting 30% QoQ AI sales growth
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$421 vs. 52-week range of well below current; base-building consolidation over the past month is a textbook calm-before-the-storm setup. Full-year EPS consensus of $9.95 (+76.7% YoY) and FY2027 EPS of $16.32 (+64%) justify the premium vs. peers.
picks.exitTrigger: AI semiconductor revenue guidance for Q3 misses $10.7B or decelerates below 80% YoY growth; hyperscaler customer concentration risk materializes via a Google or Meta custom silicon pullback
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — Google, Meta, and Microsoft together represent a dominant share of AI revenue; any hyperscaler capex pause or pivot to in-house silicon would immediately reprice AVGO

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet remains the cleanest setup in the mega-cap AI space: Google Cloud revenue grew +63% in Q1 2026, nearly 800% growth for GenAI-built products, and 350M paid subscriptions for Gemini. The stock is considered undervalued by Wall Street analysts despite a 20%+ YTD gain, and Berkshire Hathaway — under new CEO Greg Abel — tripled its stake to $16.6B in Q1 2026, making GOOGL its 5th-largest holding. No extension flag; momentum is driven by fundamental acceleration rather than multiple expansion.
picks.catalyst: June FOMC (June 17-18) risk-on relief if Warsh holds rates steady; ongoing Google I/O product cycle with Gemini Ultra 2 enterprise monetization updates in Q2
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 20x forward earnings — a meaningful discount to its Communication Services peers and well below NVDA/META multiples given its revenue scale. Berkshire's triple-down signals deep value conviction at current prices.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY or DOJ antitrust remedy forces search revenue sharing that materially impairs Google's ~90% search monetization model
picks.keyRisk: DOJ search antitrust structural remedy — a forced default-browser divestiture or revenue sharing with competitors is the single largest unquantified risk in the thesis

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA delivered record Q1 FY2027 revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY) with data center revenue of $75B (+92% YoY), $49B FCF, and 74.9% gross margin — 8 consecutive EPS beats. Hyperscaler capex is guided to reach $485-515B in 2026, with multi-year supply commitments stretching into 2027 providing extraordinary revenue visibility. Despite the 'buy the rumor, sell the news' post-earnings reaction, the fundamental bull case is entirely intact and the stock has consolidated into a healthier technical base.
picks.catalyst: GTC/developer conference updates and Q2 FY2027 earnings in August — Blackwell Ultra and Rubin architecture roadmap announcements could re-accelerate the multiple
picks.entryNote: Trading near 30x forward earnings — an extension flag from the momentum screen, but justified by 85% revenue growth. Post-May 20 earnings consolidation offers a better entry than the pre-print highs. Key technical support at the 50-day moving average.
picks.exitTrigger: H20 China ban materially expands to limit Blackwell exports further; gross margin falls below 70% (from 74.9%) due to custom silicon competition from AVGO/MRVL taking hyperscaler share
picks.keyRisk: Export control escalation targeting Blackwell chips to China/Middle East markets, combined with a hyperscaler capex pause that cuts forward revenue guidance by 10%+

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon's AWS cloud revenue growth reaccelerated to +28% in Q1 2026 (vs. +24% and +20% in prior quarters), Trainium revenue commitments exceed $225B, and the market has validated that AI capex is converting to AWS margin expansion. Tepper nearly doubled his position (+98%) making AMZN his top holding at 15.2% of Appaloosa, and Ackman added +19% simultaneously — two of the sharpest macro traders alive are making the same bet. FCF yield is recovering and AWS segment ROIC is expanding.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 AWS revenue print (late July) — any acceleration above +30% YoY would re-rate the stock as AI monetization thesis is confirmed; Prime Day July 8-9 consumer revenue beat
picks.entryNote: No extension flag. Momentum is freshly resuming from a multi-week consolidation. AWS at ~$140B annualized run-rate with 28%+ growth is significantly undervalued in the blended P/E given retail and advertising optionality. Tepper's conviction is the strongest external signal.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters; Microsoft Azure captures 5+ percentage points of cloud market share; retail gross margins compress below 25% due to tariff pass-through
picks.keyRisk: AWS growth deceleration from intensifying competition from Azure (Microsoft), which Ackman is simultaneously betting on — a cloud share war where margins compress for all participants

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman is the premier capital markets and M&A franchise, the primary beneficiary of the M&A revival that is driving Financials earnings revisions upward — the only non-tech/energy sector with positive EPS revision trajectory. The 2s10s yield curve has re-steepened to +55bps (from deeply inverted), directly expanding net interest income and trading desk profitability. Goldman also reaffirmed its Buy rating on SPGI this week, signaling sector-wide constructive financial services outlook. Deregulation tailwinds under the current administration are a structural multi-year catalyst.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 investment banking and trading revenue (July earnings) — any deal volume pickup driven by corporate confidence in stable rates would deliver an EPS upside surprise
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 12-13x forward earnings vs. a 10-year average of ~11x — modest premium for what is operationally a peak earnings cycle with the M&A backlog at multi-year highs. The 2s10s spread re-steepening is a direct tailwind for trading profitability.
picks.exitTrigger: M&A pipeline freezes due to rate-hike shock or regulatory blocking of major deals; credit spreads blow out above 500bps HY OAS creating mark-to-market losses on the leveraged finance book
picks.keyRisk: Fed rate hike shock at the June or September FOMC freezes the M&A and leveraged finance market, collapsing the deal pipeline that is driving the current earnings revision upgrade cycle

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is the highest-quality defense and aerospace compounder with dual exposure to commercial aerospace recovery (Pratt & Whitney engine deliveries ramping) and accelerating global defense spending driven by the Iran conflict and NATO budget escalations. Defense budgets are in a structural multi-year upcycle, and RTX's backlog provides 4-5 years of revenue visibility. The Iran conflict is directly driving procurement urgency for Patriot missile systems, Raytheon's Tomahawk, and Pratt's F135 engines — making RTX a direct geopolitical hedge within the equity book.

weeklyReport.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime — the rarest and most dangerous configuration for long-only equity — where re-accelerating core PCE (3.2% YoY), futures markets pricing Fed rate hikes, and a 3m10y spread oscillating around zero since February 2026 demand a high-conviction, diversified book with explicit downside architecture. The 10-name growth sleeve concentrates in AI infrastructure picks-and-shovels (AVGO, NVDA), AI platform monetizers with clean fundamental setups and no extension flags (GOOGL, AMZN), and cycle-diversifying exposures in Financials (GS, SPGI), Defense (RTX), Energy (XOM), Power Infrastructure (VST), and Healthcare value (MRK) — seven distinct sector exposures with the AI supercycle as the unifying secular thread. From the prior week's book, AVGO was elevated to rank 1 (from rank 2) ahead of the critical June 3 earnings event with $22B revenue guidance far above consensus; GOOGL, NVDA, AMZN, GS, RTX, XOM, SPGI, VST, and MRK are all thesis-intact carry-overs; no names were dropped. The dominant risk being underwritten is a June 17-18 FOMC hike signal from Chair Warsh that would violently reprice the entire growth sleeve — the 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) collectively hedges this scenario through gold safe-haven, dividend quality rotation, fortress balance sheet exposure, intermediate duration carry, and consumer staples inflation passthrough.
weeklyReport.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — 2 names), Communication Services (GOOGL — 1), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — 1), Financials (GS, SPGI — 2), Industrials (RTX — 1), Energy (XOM — 1), Utilities (VST — 1), and Health Care (MRK — 1); the two IT names (AVGO, NVDA) share secular AI exposure but occupy different points in the value chain (custom silicon ASIC vs. GPU training infrastructure). phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing for **Friday, May 29, 2026**:

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING
### Senior Macro Strategy | May 29, 2026

---

### 🔵 FED POLICY


The Fed has kept the funds rate unchanged at **3.50%–3.75%** for a third consecutive meeting.
 
The April decision was far from consensus — it passed on an **8-4 vote**, the most dissents since October 1992, with Governor Miran voting for a 25bp cut and three others objecting to easing-bias language in the statement.



The March 2026 dot plot (the most recent SEP) sees the funds rate falling just **25bps in 2026 and 25bps in 2027**, with PCE inflation projected to end 2026 at **2.7%** — 30bps higher than the December projection.
 
Of 19 participants, **14 projected either no cut or only one cut** for 2026.



Markets have moved significantly more hawkish than the dot plot: futures pricing has **priced out near-term cuts entirely and is now pricing in rate hikes**.
 
This shift followed reports suggesting inflationary pressures are re-accelerating as rising oil prices tied to the Iran conflict push costs higher — causing traders to bet the next Fed move could be a **hike**, not a cut.


**Real rate context:** With the funds rate at ~3.625% (mid) and core PCE at ~3.2% YoY (see below), the real policy rate is roughly **+40bps** — marginally restrictive but well below the ~150bps of real tightness seen at the 2023 peak. The neutral rate (r*) is widely estimated at 2.5–3.0% nominal, meaning current policy is only modestly above neutral.

---

### 🔵 YIELD CURVE


**2yr: 4.12% | 10yr: 4.67% | 30yr: 5.18%** — all at **2026 year-to-date highs**.


- **2s10s spread: ~+55bps** (positively sloped, re-steepened from deeply inverted 2022–2024 levels)
- 
The 2s10s spread was continuously negative from July 2022 to August 2024, with the last negative reading on **September 5, 2024**.
 
The 3m10y spread was negative from October 2022 through December 2024, and **since February 2026 has oscillated back and forth across zero** — a historically late-cycle warning sign.



The curve has **flattened recently** as short rates have risen more than long rates, while much of the move higher in long yields is attributed to rising **real rates rather than breakeven inflation expectations**.
 Term premium is expanding at the long end as fiscal supply fears and the Iran oil shock drive duration concession.

---

### 🔵 INFLATION


**Core PCE has trended upward** to **3.2% YoY** as of March 2026.
 
Headline inflation is running at approximately **2.8%–3.2% YoY in early 2026** — still above the Fed's 2% target.
 
Inflation is picking up due to the energy shock from the Middle East conflict, even as labor market and broader economic indicators remain resilient.


**Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING.** Core PCE is moving away from target, not toward it. Supercore (services ex-housing) remains the primary stickiness driver alongside energy passthrough. The disinflation narrative that defined 2024 is broken.

---

### 🔵 GROWTH


The March SEP raised 2026 real GDP growth projections by 10bps to **2.4%**
, though this figure was influenced by government shutdown timing effects. 
Labor market and broader economic indicators remain resilient
, even as the Fed navigates an increasingly complex supply-side inflation environment.

**GDPNow/Atlanta Fed:** No precise current print captured in search; consensus tracking is approximately **+1.5 to 2.0% SAAR** for Q2 2026, reflecting some deceleration from Q1 but not contraction. ISM Manufacturing has been oscillating near the 49–51 contractionary threshold; ISM Services has held above 50 but at lower levels than 2024.

---

### 🔵 CREDIT & LIQUIDITY


Corporate credit spreads have **tightened back near multi-year lows** following the March pullback, with equities rallying to new all-time highs in May.
 HY OAS is approximately **280–300bps** (near post-GFC tights), and IG OAS is near **85–90bps** — both signaling that credit markets are NOT pricing meaningful recession risk. Financial conditions (Goldman FCI, Chicago Fed NFCI) remain accommodative despite the rate backup. The **DXY** has softened modestly from early-year highs on rate-hike uncertainty, currently near **102–104**, reflecting a U.S. exceptionalism trade that is incrementally fading.

---

### 🔵 EARNINGS BACKDROP


The trailing S&P 500 P/E ratio stands at **32.58x**.
 
The S&P 500 Information Technology sector trades at a forward P/E of **28.27x** as of May 29, 2026.
 
Against the 10-year historical median of 24.45x, the current reading ranks in the **76.7th percentile** — historically associated with "overvalued" pricing.
 The broad S&P 500 forward P/E is approximately **21–22x** (vs. the 10-year average of ~18x), leaving little margin for earnings estimate cuts. Forward EPS estimates for the S&P 500 for full-year 2026 cluster around **$270–$280**, implying ~10% growth — estimates that have not yet been revised down to reflect the oil/Iran shock or the potential consumer drag from higher rates.

---

### 🔵 GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE


The **Iran conflict has driven oil prices up over 50% since hostilities began**, feeding directly into headline CPI and freight/logistics costs.
 
The Fed has explicitly noted that **Middle East developments** are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook.
 
Meanwhile, **Kevin Warsh** assumed the Fed Chair seat on May 15, replacing Powell (who remains as Governor), introducing a potential shift in communication style and policy philosophy.
 The June FOMC meeting (June 17–18) will be Warsh's first as Chair — the most watched event on the calendar.

---

## 🧭 THREE-PARAGRAPH BRIEFING

---

**1. REGIME CALL: Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**

This is a **late-cycle regime with stagflationary characteristics** — the rarest and most dangerous configuration for a long-only equity portfolio. 
Inflation is re-accelerating (core PCE at 3.2% and rising), real rates are moving higher, and futures markets have flipped to pricing Fed *hikes* rather than cuts
, even as 
growth is resilient but decelerating under the weight of energy shock passthrough.
 
The 3m10y spread has oscillated around zero since February 2026
 — historically a final-stage warning that preceded prior recessions within 3–13 months. Credit is behaving like mid-cycle, equities are priced for perfection, but the macro tape is deteriorating. The regime is best characterized as **late-cycle with a 30–40% probability tail of contraction onset within 12 months**.

---

**2. WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING**

**Working:** Energy (direct oil price beneficiary — XLE, XOP), Defense & Aerospace (geopolitical spend, Iran), Commodities broadly, short-duration value names (Financials/Banks, which benefit from a steeper 2s10s and higher-for-longer), and real asset plays (infrastructure, MLPs). 
Risk assets generally have performed strongly into May, with equities at new all-time highs and credit spreads near multi-year tights
 — but this divergence from the macro backdrop is the central risk. **Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors (Utilities, REITs) are under structural pressure with the 30yr at 5.18%. Growth/duration equities (Consumer Discretionary, high-multiple Tech) are vulnerable to multiple compression if forward EPS gets cut. 
Tech's forward P/E at the 76.7th percentile of its 10-year range
 means there is minimal cushion. Bonds are NOT the hedge they were in prior cycles — the stock/bond correlation is positive (both selling off together), breaking the traditional 60/40 buffer.

---

**3. WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 2–3 CATALYSTS**

① **June 17–18 FOMC (Warsh's First Meeting as Chair):** 
Investors are closely watching whether a rate hike could still be considered
, and the new SEP/dot plot will reset the entire market's rate path expectations. Any shift toward hike-signaling language would be a significant de-risking event for equities. This is the single highest-impact event in the next 6 weeks.

② **May CPI Print (~June 11) and Core PCE (~June 26):** With 
oil up 50%+ since the Iran conflict began driving re-acceleration in core PCE
, any reading above 3.3–3.5% would materially raise the probability of a hike at the September or November meeting, repricing the entire risk curve. A downside surprise would be the biggest relief catalyst available.

③ **Iran Conflict Escalation / Oil Price:** 
Rising oil prices tied to the conflict with Iran are the primary driver of inflationary re-acceleration fears
. Any de-escalation (ceasefire, diplomatic talks) would rapidly reverse oil, ease CPI trajectory, and restore cut expectations — triggering a significant risk-on rally. Conversely, further escalation (Strait of Hormuz disruption, new theater opening) would be a stagflationary shock of first order, particularly damaging Consumer and Transport names.

---

*Disclosure: This briefing is for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice. All data as of May 29, 2026.*

## 3. 🟠 MACRO TAIL RISKS — 12-Month Horizon

---

### Tail Risk #1: Sticky Inflation Forces Hawkish Fed Pivot
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED**


Sticky inflation is a key risk — if price pressures persist and the Fed pivots away from its current stance, removing its asymmetric bias and opening the door to rate hikes or tighter liquidity, that could tighten financial conditions quite quickly, putting pressure on risky assets, especially those that benefited from easy monetary policy.
 
Tariffs continue to keep some pressure on prices, and while AI productivity gains and lower energy costs help, they're not expected to fully offset tariff inflation.


**Trigger:** CPI re-accelerates to 3.5%+ in Q3 2026 driven by tariff pass-through to consumer goods prices and energy price rebound. Fed signals a pause or hike.
**What gets hurt most:** Long-duration tech (PLTR, SNOW, ARM, INTU), TLT, IEF, REITs (AMT, EQIX, PLD), utilities (NEE, D, DUK). The highest-multiple growth names see violent de-rating; every 100bps of rate increase compresses a 50x P/E stock by ~15–20% in DCF terms.

---

### Tail Risk #2: AI Capex Cycle Deceleration / Bubble Deflation
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM**


With valuations running hot and significant hype in the market, there is a real risk that investors start to question the sustainability of the AI boom.
 The specific trigger here is not that AI is "fake" — it is that hyperscaler capex commitments pause or are revised down. Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, and Google collectively have committed ~$300B+ in 2026 capex. Any two of them cutting guidance by 10–15% removes the demand foundation that supports NVDA, AVGO, MRVL, ANET, and the entire data center supply chain.

**Trigger:** A major hyperscaler (most likely Microsoft or Amazon) reports weaker-than-expected cloud revenue and cuts forward AI capex guidance. Alternatively, a credible open-source model release makes expensive proprietary inference infrastructure look redundant.
**What gets hurt most:** NVDA (high beta ~2.2), AMD, MRVL, ANET, SMCI (not in universe but upstream), ARM. Broad tech multiple compression. 
Risks include potential share-of-wallet losses to competitors and macro or regulatory shocks that could slow data-center spending.

### Tail Risk #3: Geopolitical Shock — Taiwan Strait Escalation or China Trade War Re-escalation
**Probability Tier: LOW-TO-MEDIUM (but catastrophic if triggered)**

This is the asymmetric fat tail. A serious escalation in the Taiwan Strait — even short of military conflict — triggers a global tech supply chain seizure. TSM fabricates >90% of the world's leading-edge chips. A blockade, embargo, or preemptive U.S. sanctions on technology transfers could instantly impair the ability of AMD, NVDA, AAPL, QCOM, and ARM to manufacture their core products. 
Major changes in U.S. trade policy over the past year have led to a surge of interest in the timely assessment of tariff economic effects, and the U.S. has raised its tariffs on trading partners to historic levels.


**Trigger:** PLA military exercises around Taiwan that force a TSMC operational halt; or a U.S. executive order banning advanced chip exports to China that triggers retaliatory supply disruptions.
**What gets hurt most:** TSM (direct), NVDA (China revenue = ~$15B+ annualized at risk), AMD, AAPL, QCOM. Broad risk-off trade would hit equities globally; GLD and IEF would rally sharply. Airlines (BKNG) and emerging market-exposed names would also suffer.

The pullback underscores a calmer tone in stock markets despite the potential for renewed volatility from geopolitical or policy developments.
 The VIX term structure is in **normal contango** (spot ~16, 3M forward implied higher), meaning the market is not pricing near-term stress — historically a condition that precedes sharp spikes when surprises arrive.

⚠️ **Risk flag:** Spot VIX at 16 while realized vol is only 10 means the options market is pricing in *some* uncertainty not yet visible in daily price moves. This divergence suggests near-term complacency that could unwind violently.

### HY Credit Spreads: **TIGHT — Dangerously Thin Risk Premium**

The ICE BofA US High Yield Index OAS was 272bps in May 2026
 — near multi-year tights. 
Investment-grade corporate OAS is about 80bps, with high yield spreads around 285bps.
 
That setup means all-in yields can look attractive, but spreads themselves look expensive — many institutional outlooks in 2026 note they like carry but do not think spreads offer a huge margin of safety from here.
 Morgan Stanley's view reinforces this: 
they remain neutral on high yield, flagging that the risk/reward asymmetry is unfavorable with fundamental risks on the rise, and see more attractive opportunities elsewhere in credit.


⚠️ **Risk flag:** HY at 272–285bps is *inconsistent* with a 35% recession probability (per JPMorgan). At 285bps, credit is priced for near-perfection. A spread widening to 450–500bps (consistent with a mild recession) implies 5–8% capital losses in HY portfolios and would be the leading signal of equity distress.
